meta.title: Hydrogen Storage Technology Comparator dataset
meta.description: Curated physical, operating and economic parameters for hydrogen storage technologies, compiled from peer-reviewed literature, DOE program records and techno-economic studies.
meta.basis_note: Gravimetric capacity is reported on a 'storage medium' basis for chemical carriers and metal hydrides (mass H2 / mass carrier, excluding tank, reactor or balance-of-plant), and on a 'system' basis for compressed and liquid hydrogen (mass H2 / mass of gas + pressure vessel or cryostat), because compressed/liquid H2 has no carrier material of its own. This distinction matters and is called out explicitly so the comparison is not misleading.

technologies.name: Compressed gas H2, 350 bar
technologies.cost_note: Aboveground tank LCOS roughly 0.3-1.2 USD/kg H2 cycled, depending on duty cycle
technologies.notes: Mature technology used for tube trailers, buses and trucks. Energy density still well below the DOE system targets.
technologies.source: Demaco Cryogenics, 'The energy density of hydrogen' (2025); Sustainable Energy Fuels TEA review (2022)

technologies.name: Compressed gas H2, 700 bar
technologies.cost_note: Higher CAPEX per kg than 350 bar tanks due to thicker composite wrap
technologies.notes: Standard for fuel cell passenger cars (e.g. Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO class vehicles).
technologies.source: Demaco Cryogenics (2025); ScienceDirect 'Compressed Hydrogen - an overview' topic page

technologies.name: Liquid hydrogen (cryogenic)
technologies.gravimetric_basis: system, strongly scale-dependent (insulation mass dominates at small scale)
technologies.cost_note: Liquefaction alone consumes about 12-15.5 kWh per kg H2, roughly 35-45% of the H2 lower heating value
technologies.notes: Highest volumetric density of any non-chemical option, but boil-off losses and liquefaction energy penalty are the real cost driver.
technologies.source: Demaco Cryogenics (2025); Liu et al., Particle & Particle Systems Characterization (2025)

technologies.name: Ammonia (liquid carrier)
technologies.gravimetric_basis: neat carrier (NH3 mass basis), before cracking losses
technologies.cost_note: Ammonia storage/shipping is mature and cheap; the cracking step to release pure H2 is the less mature, more expensive part
technologies.notes: Best volumetric and gravimetric density of the carriers shown here, but recovering pure H2 needs an additional cracking and purification process not required for LOHC.
technologies.source: BBA Consultants, 'Ammonia vs dibenzyltoluene' comparative assessment (2025)

technologies.name: LOHC (dibenzyltoluene / H18-DBT)
technologies.gravimetric_basis: neat carrier, theoretical loading (6.0 wt% practical once incomplete dehydrogenation is accounted for)
technologies.cost_note: Carrier itself is cheap (~4 EUR/kg DBT) and reusable for many cycles, but dehydrogenation needs high-grade heat (270-320 degC)
technologies.notes: Demonstrated at pilot/early-commercial scale (e.g. Hydrogenious). Liquid at ambient conditions, so it can use existing fuel logistics infrastructure.
technologies.source: Wikipedia 'Liquid organic hydrogen carrier'; PMC review 'Development of LOHCs for Hydrogen Storage and Transport' (2024); BBA Consultants (2025)

technologies.name: Metal hydride - MgH2
technologies.cost_note: Mg is abundant and cheap, but the high desorption temperature drives significant system-level heat-management cost
technologies.notes: Best volumetric density of the solid-state options shown here. Slow kinetics without catalysts/nanostructuring is the main practical barrier.
technologies.source: Nizam et al., Asia-Pacific Journal of Chemical Engineering (2026); Wiley Particle & Particle Systems Characterization review (2025)

technologies.name: Metal hydride - NaAlH4 (Ti-doped)
technologies.volumetric_basis: estimated from material density (~1.28 g/cm3) x gravimetric capacity
technologies.cost_note: Lower desorption temperature than MgH2 is an advantage, but cycling stability and Ti-catalyst cost limited commercialisation
technologies.notes: One of the most extensively studied complex hydrides; a useful reference point even though it never reached large-scale deployment.
technologies.source: Wiley Particle & Particle Systems Characterization review (2025); US patent 'Doped hydrogen storage material' (8,444,876)

technologies.name: Metal hydride - LaNi5 (intermetallic)
technologies.volumetric_basis: estimated from intermetallic density x gravimetric capacity
technologies.cost_note: Mature for niche stationary/backup use, but La and Ni content add material cost and supply-chain exposure
technologies.notes: Poor gravimetric density but fast kinetics and room-temperature operation made it the first metal hydride used commercially, in stationary buffer and backup-power niches.
technologies.source: Wiley Particle & Particle Systems Characterization review (2025); Wikipedia 'Hydrogen storage'

technologies.name: Underground salt cavern (bulk gas)
technologies.gravimetric_basis: not applicable - bulk geological storage, not a packaged system
technologies.cost_note: Levelized cost of storage roughly 0.14-1.75 USD/kg H2 depending on cycle frequency and site; cheapest known large-scale option
technologies.notes: Not comparable on a per-kg density basis since it is a geological formation rather than a packaged storage unit, but included because it is the benchmark for seasonal, large-scale hydrogen storage in Germany.
technologies.source: EWI Cologne, 'EWI analyses costs for underground hydrogen storage' (2025); Papadias & Ahluwalia, ANL/OSTI bulk storage report

reference_targets.name: DOE 2025 system target
reference_targets.source: US DOE Hydrogen Program Record / onboard storage target explanation document
